content.text: A single-turn interaction is the simplest unit of LLM use: one request containing a system prompt and a user message, one response, and no continuation. The application does not maintain conversation history between calls. This makes single-turn interactions fast to implement, cheap to run, stateless to scale, and straightforward to test — and they are the right architecture for a surprisingly large fraction of real LLM workloads.
Single-Turn vs Multi-Turn: Architecture Comparison

attrs.alt: Left: single-turn stateless flow — request (system + user) sent to LLM API, response returned, no state stored. Each request is independent. Right: multi-turn stateful flow — each request resends the entire conversation history (all prior user and assistant turns); client maintains state; context and cost grow each turn. Single-turn suits classification, extraction, transformation. Multi-turn suits support chat, tutoring, task agents.

content.text: In a single-turn interaction, the context for the response must be self-contained in the single request. There is no "remember what I said earlier" — if the model needs background information, it must be in the current prompt. This constraint is a feature, not a limitation: it forces prompt design to be explicit and complete, which makes behaviour more predictable.
When to Use Single-Turn
Task type
Example
Why single-turn fits
Classification
Sentiment, intent, category, priority
All context (the text to classify) is in the request; no prior exchange needed
Extraction
Named entities, dates, prices, structured fields from text
Source text is self-contained; output is deterministic structured data
Transformation
Summarise, translate, rewrite, format conversion
Input and output are the same document in different forms; no conversation needed
Generation
Write a product description, email, commit message, docstring
All spec is provided upfront; iterative refinement not needed (or handled externally)
Fact lookup
"What is the capital of France?" or FAQ answering
Question and any context injected in the single prompt
Routing / triage
Assign incoming ticket to a department or priority
High-volume, low-cost; each ticket is an independent request
Basic Single-Turn Implementation

import anthropic

client = anthropic.Anthropic()

def single_turn(system: str, user_message: str, model: str = "claude-haiku-4-5-20251001", max_tokens: int = 256) -> str:
    """Execute a single stateless LLM call."""
    response = client.messages.create(
        model=model,
        max_tokens=max_tokens,
        system=system,
        messages=[
            {"role": "user", "content": user_message}
        ],
    )
    return response.content[0].text

# Usage: same function, different system prompts for different tasks
sentiment = single_turn(
    system="Classify the sentiment of the review. Reply with exactly one word: positive, negative, or neutral.",
    user_message="Shipping was slow but the product quality exceeded my expectations.",
    max_tokens=8,
)
print(sentiment)  # "positive"

summary = single_turn(
    system="Summarise the text in one sentence.",
    user_message="Large language models are trained on vast datasets...",
    max_tokens=64,
)
print(summary)
System Prompt Design for Single-Turn Tasks

Because single-turn interactions have no conversation to fall back on, the system prompt bears the full weight of defining task, format, constraints, and edge-case behaviour. A well-structured system prompt for single-turn tasks typically has three components:
Role / persona
— what the model is and what it knows ("You are a senior customer support agent for AcmeCorp who knows the return policy and product catalog").
Task specification
— exactly what to do and what not to do ("Classify the customer message as: billing | returns | shipping | other. Reply with the category only. Do not explain.").
Output format
— the exact structure expected ("Reply with a single word from the allowed list", or "Reply with valid JSON matching: {\"category\": string, \"confidence\": 0–1}").

import anthropic
import json

client = anthropic.Anthropic()

TRIAGE_SYSTEM = """\
You are a customer support triage agent for AcmeCorp.

Task: Classify each incoming message into exactly one of these categories:
  billing     — invoices, payment failures, subscription changes
  returns     — refund requests, return authorisations
  shipping    — delivery status, missing packages, address changes
  technical   — product faults, setup issues, bugs
  other       — anything that doesn't fit the above

Output format: Respond with valid JSON only, no preamble:
{"category": "<category>", "urgency": "low|medium|high"}

Urgency rules:
  high   — angry tone, explicit complaint, deadline mentioned, order value > $500 implied
  medium — neutral tone, clear problem
  low    — general enquiry, no urgency signal"""

def triage_message(customer_message: str) -> dict:
    response = client.messages.create(
        model="claude-haiku-4-5-20251001",
        max_tokens=48,
        system=TRIAGE_SYSTEM,
        messages=[{"role": "user", "content": customer_message}],
    )
    return json.loads(response.content[0].text)

result = triage_message("I was charged twice for my subscription this month! Fix this NOW.")
print(result)  # {'category': 'billing', 'urgency': 'high'}
Common Single-Turn Patterns
Structured Data Extraction
Extract structured fields from unstructured text. The system prompt specifies the schema; the user message provides the raw text.

import anthropic
import json
from dataclasses import dataclass

client = anthropic.Anthropic()

@dataclass
class ContactInfo:
    name: str
    email: str | None
    phone: str | None
    company: str | None

EXTRACT_SYSTEM = """\
Extract contact information from the text.
Output valid JSON matching exactly:
{"name": string|null, "email": string|null, "phone": string|null, "company": string|null}
If a field is not present, use null. No other output."""

def extract_contact(text: str) -> ContactInfo:
    response = client.messages.create(
        model="claude-haiku-4-5-20251001",
        max_tokens=128,
        system=EXTRACT_SYSTEM,
        messages=[{"role": "user", "content": text}],
    )
    data = json.loads(response.content[0].text)
    return ContactInfo(**data)

contact = extract_contact(
    "Hi, I'm Sarah Chen from Acme Corp. You can reach me at sarah.chen@acme.io or +1-415-555-0192."
)
print(contact)
# ContactInfo(name='Sarah Chen', email='sarah.chen@acme.io',
#             phone='+1-415-555-0192', company='Acme Corp')
Document Transformation

import anthropic

client = anthropic.Anthropic()

def summarise(text: str, max_sentences: int = 3) -> str:
    """Single-turn summarisation with configurable length."""
    response = client.messages.create(
        model="claude-haiku-4-5-20251001",
        max_tokens=256,
        system=f"Summarise the text in {max_sentences} sentences. No preamble.",
        messages=[{"role": "user", "content": text}],
    )
    return response.content[0].text

def translate(text: str, target_language: str) -> str:
    """Single-turn translation."""
    response = client.messages.create(
        model="claude-haiku-4-5-20251001",
        max_tokens=len(text.split()) * 3,  # rough output budget
        system=f"Translate the text to {target_language}. Output only the translation, no explanation.",
        messages=[{"role": "user", "content": text}],
    )
    return response.content[0].text

def improve_grammar(text: str) -> str:
    """Single-turn grammar and style correction."""
    response = client.messages.create(
        model="claude-haiku-4-5-20251001",
        max_tokens=len(text.split()) * 3,
        system="Correct grammar and style errors. Preserve the author's voice. Output only the corrected text.",
        messages=[{"role": "user", "content": text}],
    )
    return response.content[0].text
One-Shot Text Generation

import anthropic

client = anthropic.Anthropic()

def write_commit_message(diff: str) -> str:
    """Generate a conventional commit message from a git diff."""
    response = client.messages.create(
        model="claude-haiku-4-5-20251001",
        max_tokens=64,
        system="""Write a git commit message in Conventional Commits format.
Format: <type>(<scope>): <description>
Types: feat, fix, docs, refactor, test, chore.
First line only. Max 72 characters. No body. No bullet points.""",
        messages=[{"role": "user", "content": f"Git diff:\n{diff}"}],
    )
    return response.content[0].text.strip()

def write_product_description(product_name: str, features: list[str]) -> str:
    """Generate a short product description from feature list."""
    feature_list = "\n".join(f"- {f}" for f in features)
    response = client.messages.create(
        model="claude-haiku-4-5-20251001",
        max_tokens=128,
        system="Write a compelling 2-sentence product description. No preamble, just the description.",
        messages=[
            {"role": "user", "content": f"Product: {product_name}\nFeatures:\n{feature_list}"},
        ],
    )
    return response.content[0].text
Batch Processing Single-Turn Tasks
Since single-turn requests are independent, they are trivially parallelisable. Run many concurrently with
asyncio.gather()
to saturate rate limits and minimise total wall-clock time.
Testing Single-Turn Interactions

Single-turn interactions are the easiest type to test: a fixed input produces a response that can be evaluated against expected outputs or via an LLM judge. Write an eval harness that runs a test set and reports pass rate.

import anthropic
from dataclasses import dataclass

client = anthropic.Anthropic()

@dataclass
class TestCase:
    input: str
    expected: str  # exact match or substring
    match_type: str = "exact"  # "exact" | "contains" | "startswith"

def run_eval(system: str, test_cases: list[TestCase], model: str = "claude-haiku-4-5-20251001") -> dict:
    passed = 0
    failures = []

    for tc in test_cases:
        response = client.messages.create(
            model=model,
            max_tokens=32,
            system=system,
            messages=[{"role": "user", "content": tc.input}],
        )
        actual = response.content[0].text.strip().lower()
        expected = tc.expected.lower()

        ok = (
            actual == expected                    if tc.match_type == "exact" else
            expected in actual                    if tc.match_type == "contains" else
            actual.startswith(expected)
        )
        if ok:
            passed += 1
        else:
            failures.append({"input": tc.input, "expected": tc.expected, "actual": actual})

    return {
        "pass_rate": passed / len(test_cases),
        "passed": passed,
        "total": len(test_cases),
        "failures": failures,
    }

# Example eval for sentiment classifier
test_cases = [
    TestCase("I love this!", "positive"),
    TestCase("Terrible experience.", "negative"),
    TestCase("It arrived on time.", "neutral"),
    TestCase("Completely broken out of the box.", "negative"),
]
result = run_eval(
    system="Classify sentiment as positive, negative, or neutral. One word only.",
    test_cases=test_cases,
)
print(result)
# {'pass_rate': 1.0, 'passed': 4, 'total': 4, 'failures': []}
Common Mistakes
Using multi-turn architecture when single-turn suffices.
Adding unnecessary conversation history management to a classification pipeline adds latency, cost, and complexity. If each request is self-contained, keep it stateless.
Underspecified system prompts.

A vague system prompt like "classify this" produces inconsistent output formats and labels across different inputs. Single-turn prompts need to specify allowed outputs, format, and edge cases explicitly — there is no conversation to clarify.
Setting max_tokens too high for short-output tasks.

A single-word classifier with max_tokens=1024 will often produce explanations and qualifications instead of just the label. Set max_tokens tightly and instruct the model to output only the expected format.
Not testing with real production inputs.
Clean, well-formed test inputs are easy. Production inputs include typos, partial sentences, code-switching, emoji, and adversarial phrasing. Test on a random sample of real data before deploying.
Ignoring the temperature parameter for deterministic tasks.
Classification and extraction should use a low temperature (0.0–0.2) to maximise consistency. The default temperature on most APIs is 1.0, which introduces unnecessary variance.
